description:
You are a data-scientist who has inherited a “raw” dump of CSV datasets that contains many outdated “_backup.csv” files mixed in with current data.  
Your goal is to isolate every “_backup.csv” file, store them (compressed) in a separate backup tree, and leave behind a verifiable audit trail.

Initial layout that already exists before you start (all paths are absolute):

/home/user/datasets/raw/  
├── sales_jan.csv  
├── sales_feb.csv  
├── customers.csv  
├── sales_backup.csv  
├── readme.txt  
├── old/  
│   ├── sales_jan_backup.csv  
│   └── customers_backup.csv  
└── archive/  
    └── 2021_sales_backup.csv  

No other files or directories exist under /home/user/datasets/ at start-time.

The work you must do:

1. Create the directory /home/user/datasets/backup (if it does not yet exist) and replicate inside it the same relative directory hierarchy that each “_backup.csv” file originally lived in.  
   Example: “/home/user/datasets/raw/old/sales_jan_backup.csv” must end up as “/home/user/datasets/backup/old/sales_jan_backup.csv.gz”.

2. Locate every file whose name ends exactly with “_backup.csv” anywhere **under** /home/user/datasets/raw (any depth).  
   • You must use a Unix `find … -type f … -name '*_backup.csv'` style command piped through `xargs` (or the equivalent `-exec … +`) to perform the batch processing.  
   • Each located file must be moved to the proper place under /home/user/datasets/backup, then compressed with gzip so the final on-disk file is “original_name_backup.csv.gz”.  
   • After the operation, no plain “_backup.csv” files should remain anywhere under /home/user/datasets/raw.

3. Produce an audit log file named
   /home/user/datasets/cleanup_logs/backup_move.log  
   (create the parent directory “cleanup_logs” if necessary).

   Log-file format that will be automatically verified (one line per handled file, **exactly** four pipe-separated fields, no extra whitespace):

   original_full_path|new_full_path|size_in_bytes_before|timestamp_ISO8601

   Field details:  
   • original_full_path – absolute path where the “_backup.csv” file originally lived.  
   • new_full_path     – absolute path (including “.gz” suffix) after it has been moved *and* compressed.  
   • size_in_bytes_before – the file size (integer, as reported by “wc -c” or “stat -c %s”) *before* compression.  
   • timestamp_ISO8601 – current timestamp when the move happens in the exact format YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS (no timezone, 24-hour clock, zero-padded).

   The log lines must be sorted in lexicographical order by original_full_path so that automated tests can do a straightforward string comparison.

   Example of one correctly-formatted line (your actual values will differ):

   /home/user/datasets/raw/archive/2021_sales_backup.csv|/home/user/datasets/backup/archive/2021_sales_backup.csv.gz|4287|2023-08-31T14:03:12

4. When the task is finished you should be able to run the following manual checks successfully (the automated grader will do the same):

   a) `find /home/user/datasets/raw -name '*_backup.csv'` returns **no** output.  
   b) `find /home/user/datasets/backup -name '*.csv.gz' | wc -l` prints “4”.  
   c) The file “/home/user/datasets/cleanup_logs/backup_move.log” exists, contains exactly 4 non-empty lines, and each line matches the “original|new|size|timestamp” pattern specified above.

You do **NOT** need root privileges.  All work happens inside /home/user.  Do not hard-code any timestamps or file sizes; collect them dynamically so the log reflects reality.

The automated evaluation will:

1. Verify the 4 expected “.csv.gz” files exist at the correct absolute paths under /home/user/datasets/backup/.  
2. Confirm no “_backup.csv” files remain under /home/user/datasets/raw/.  
3. Parse backup_move.log to ensure:  
   • exactly four lines, sorted, no duplicates, pipe-delimited with 4 fields,  
   • original_full_path fields match the four initial “_backup.csv” locations,  
   • new_full_path fields match the four final “.csv.gz” locations,  
   • size_in_bytes_before is a positive integer,  
   • timestamp_ISO8601 matches the regex “^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}$”.

Use the standard GNU coreutils plus find(1), xargs(1), mkdir(1), gzip(1), and any shell scripting you like.  No Python/Ruby/etc. is necessary.

Remember:  
• Work only inside /home/user.  
• Preserve sub-directory structure when moving files.  
• Compress *after* moving.  
• Produce the audit log with the exact format above.  
• Sort the log lines.

Good luck!
truth:
Initial files that exist before the agent starts:

/home/user/datasets/raw/sales_backup.csv              – text file, 3 KB  
/home/user/datasets/raw/old/sales_jan_backup.csv      – text file, 2 KB  
/home/user/datasets/raw/old/customers_backup.csv      – text file, 1 KB  
/home/user/datasets/raw/archive/2021_sales_backup.csv – text file, 4 KB  

Non-backup files (must remain untouched):

/home/user/datasets/raw/sales_jan.csv  
/home/user/datasets/raw/sales_feb.csv  
/home/user/datasets/raw/customers.csv  
/home/user/datasets/raw/readme.txt

Directories that already exist and are writable by the normal user:

/home/user/datasets/  
/home/user/datasets/raw/  
/home/user/datasets/raw/old/  
/home/user/datasets/raw/archive/

No other directories exist under /home/user/datasets/ at start-time.

Expected final state **after** correct completion:

1. These four compressed files must now exist (gzip default compression, any level is fine):

/home/user/datasets/backup/sales_backup.csv.gz  
/home/user/datasets/backup/old/sales_jan_backup.csv.gz  
/home/user/datasets/backup/old/customers_backup.csv.gz  
/home/user/datasets/backup/archive/2021_sales_backup.csv.gz

2. Corresponding plain “_backup.csv” files have been removed from /home/user/datasets/raw and its sub-directories.

3. Directory /home/user/datasets/cleanup_logs/ exists and contains exactly one file named backup_move.log.

4. backup_move.log contains 4 lines sorted lexicographically by the first field and each line conforms to:
   <original>|<new>|<size>|<YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS>
   where
   • original ∈ {
        /home/user/datasets/raw/archive/2021_sales_backup.csv,
        /home/user/datasets/raw/old/customers_backup.csv,
        /home/user/datasets/raw/old/sales_jan_backup.csv,
        /home/user/datasets/raw/sales_backup.csv
     }
   • new is the corresponding path in /home/user/datasets/backup with “.gz” suffix.
   • size is a positive integer equal to the byte count of the original file (exact numbers known to grading code).  
   • timestamp is a 19-character string matching the stated format; grading code validates format, not the exact value.

5. All other files originally present (e.g., sales_jan.csv, sales_feb.csv, customers.csv, readme.txt) are still in their original places and unchanged.

6. No extra files or directories were created outside:
   /home/user/datasets/backup/  
   /home/user/datasets/cleanup_logs/

Permissions: every new file or directory can have default umask settings (0644 files, 0755 dirs).

These conditions are what the automated grader will assert.
